questions.question: What is the package-level variable in pocketbase.go that holds the PocketBase framework version, and what is its default (untracked build) value?
questions.gold_answer: In pocketbase.go (line 27) the exported package-level variable `var Version = "(untracked)"` holds the framework version string. Its default literal value is "(untracked)", which is intended to be overridden at build time; until then any build reports the version as "(untracked)".

questions.question: In apis/middlewares_cors.go, what is the id constant assigned to the default CORS middleware, and relative to which other middleware priority is its priority defined?
questions.gold_answer: apis/middlewares_cors.go defines `DefaultCorsMiddlewareId = "pbCors"` (line 27) and `DefaultCorsMiddlewarePriority = DefaultActivityLoggerMiddlewarePriority - 1` (line 28). The priority is one less than the activity-logger priority so the CORS middleware runs before the activity logger and rate limiter, ensuring OPTIONS preflight requests are not counted.

questions.question: Which filter modifiers does splitModifier in core/record_field_resolver.go accept as valid, and what does it return for an input with no ':' separator?
questions.gold_answer: splitModifier (core/record_field_resolver.go, line 573) splits the input on ':'. If there are not exactly two parts (i.e. no ':' separator) it returns the original combined string as the name with an empty modifier and nil error. When a modifier is present it validates it against the constant set: `isset`, `each`, `length`, `lower`, and `changed`; any other modifier yields an "unknown modifier" error.

questions.question: Describe how BaseApp.CanAccessRecord decides whether a record is accessible, including its short-circuit cases and how it applies a non-empty access rule.
questions.gold_answer: CanAccessRecord (core/record_query.go, line 599) first returns true when requestInfo.HasSuperuserAuth() is set (superusers access everything). If accessRule is nil it returns false (only superusers). If the rule is the empty string it returns true (public). Otherwise it builds a RecordQuery selecting (1) filtered to the record id, constructs a resolver via NewRecordFieldResolver(app, collection, requestInfo, true), compiles the rule with search.FilterData(*accessRule).BuildExpr(resolver), applies resolver.UpdateQuery(query), then runs the query with the expression and Limit(1), returning exists > 0. sql.ErrNoRows is tolerated.

questions.question: How does ToApiError in tools/router/error.go normalize an arbitrary error into an ApiError, and how does the resulting status depend on the wrapped error type?
questions.gold_answer: ToApiError (tools/router/error.go, line 134) uses errors.As to check whether err already wraps an *ApiError; if so that instance is returned unchanged. If not, it inspects the error: when errors.Is(err, sql.ErrNoRows) or errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist) it wraps it in NewNotFoundError (HTTP 404 / StatusNotFound); otherwise it assigns a generic NewBadRequestError (HTTP 400 / StatusBadRequest). Each constructor calls NewApiError, which builds the safe Data map via safeErrorsData and sentence-cases the message.

questions.question: In apis/middlewares_cors.go, how is each configured AllowOrigins entry (other than '*') translated into a matcher, and what happens when the pattern fails to compile?
questions.gold_answer: During CORS setup, each AllowOrigins entry that is not "*" is converted to a regular expression: the origin is escaped with regexp.QuoteMeta, then the escaped wildcards are un-escaped back into regex tokens via strings.ReplaceAll(pattern, "\\*", ".*") and strings.ReplaceAll(pattern, "\\?", "."), and the pattern is anchored as "^" + pattern + "$". It is compiled with regexp.Compile; if compilation fails the code logs "invalid AllowOrigins pattern" and continues (silently ignoring that pattern to preserve prior non-panicking behaviour) rather than crashing. Compiled patterns are collected into allowOriginPatterns.

questions.question: In core/record_field_resolver_runner.go, explain how processActiveProps and finalizeActivePropsProcessing make filter-field resolution depend on the field-path shape — covering JSON/geoPoint fields, back-relations, the nesting limit, and terminal modifiers.
questions.gold_answer: processActiveProps (line 441) iterates the split field path (r.activeProps). For the last prop it delegates to finalizeActivePropsProcessing; for intermediate props the behaviour forks by field type: a hidden non-superuser field errors as "non-filterable"; a field of type FieldTypeJSON or FieldTypeGeoPoint causes all remaining props to be treated as a JSON path (built via inflector.Columnify with numeric parts turned into [index]) and resolved with dbutils.JSONExtract. Before descending into a relation it enforces `if i >= maxNestedRels` (maxNestedRels = 6) returning "max nested relations reached". When a prop resolves to no field it is tried as a back-relation matching viaRegex `^(\w+)_via_(\w+)$`, loading the back collection/field, validating it is a RelationField whose CollectionId points back, and registering joins (multi vs single). finalizeActivePropsProcessing (line 736) calls splitModifier and branches on terminal modifiers: `:length` (lengthModifier) on a MultiValuer yields dbutils.JSONArrayLength, `:each` (eachModifier) expands into a json-each join. Missing terminal fields either return a NULL identifier (nullifyMisingField) or an "unknown field" error. Thus the exact SQL produced varies enormously with the path's field types, back-relation syntax, depth, and modifier.

questions.question: Walk through the decision logic of the anonymous CORS handler Func in apis/middlewares_cors.go: how it treats a missing Origin, preflight requests, a custom AllowOriginFunc, and the unsafe wildcard-with-credentials case.
questions.gold_answer: The returned hook.Handler's Func (starting ~line 172) reads the request Origin and always adds `Vary: Origin`. A preflight is detected as an OPTIONS request (req.Method == http.MethodOptions). If the Origin header is empty it assumes a non-browser request: it calls e.Next() for a normal request, or returns e.NoContent(StatusNoContent) for a preflight. When an Origin is present, if config.AllowOriginFunc is set it is invoked and, on allowed==true, allowOrigin is set to the origin (errors propagate). Otherwise it iterates config.AllowOrigins: a "*" entry combined with config.AllowCredentials AND config.UnsafeWildcardOriginWithAllowCredentials reflects the actual origin (the unsafe path); a plain "*" or an exact origin match sets allowOrigin (with "*" preserved unless credentials force reflection). The decision therefore hinges on Origin presence, method, the custom func, and the unsafe wildcard/credentials flags.

questions.question: How does unmarshalInStructValue in tools/router/unmarshal_request_data.go dispatch per struct field, and how does its behaviour change with the field's shape (TextUnmarshaler, slice, nested struct, slice-of-structs)?
questions.gold_answer: unmarshalInStructValue (tools/router/unmarshal_request_data.go, line 122) reflectively walks each field of dstStructValue. It skips fields tagged "-" or unexported non-anonymous fields, reads the struct tag via structTagKey to compute the lookup name, and prefixes it with structPrefix when nested. It dereferences pointer fields and detects slices (isSlice) by unwrapping reflect.Slice. Dispatch is shape-dependent: (1) if the (element) type implements encoding.TextUnmarshaler (or its pointer does) it calls UnmarshalText, building a reflect.MakeSlice for slice fields; (2) otherwise for non-struct kinds it loads values via setRegularReflectedValue, again slice-aware; (3) for nested/embedded structs it recurses into unmarshalInStructValue, updating structPrefix from the tag (or the field name); (4) a slice-of-structs is explicitly skipped because the filling rules are ambiguous / not supported. So the same function produces very different unmarshalling depending on each field's kind, tag, pointer-ness, and slice-ness.

questions.question: Filter expressions in this backend go through a resolver whose output SQL varies enormously with the field path's shape (relations, JSON fields, back-relations, modifiers). Find the two functions that implement that resolution and their file.
questions.gold_answer: processActiveProps and finalizeActivePropsProcessing in core/record_field_resolver_runner.go — risk-7 functions forking on FieldTypeJSON/geoPoint, _via_ back-relations, maxNestedRels, and :each/:length modifiers.

questions.question: Error normalization in this codebase is so load-bearing that a change to it would touch ~800+ symbols. Which function converts arbitrary errors into API errors, and in which file?
questions.gold_answer: ToApiError in tools/router/error.go (with the Is helper) — reach 800+; maps sql.ErrNoRows/fs.ErrNotExist to 404 and everything else to 400 via NewApiError.

questions.question: Somewhere in this backend's HTTP layer sits a single anonymous function flagged as one of the riskiest in the codebase: it decides cross-origin access. Which middleware file contains it, and what unsafe configuration combination does it special-case?
questions.gold_answer: The CORS handler Func in apis/middlewares_cors.go (~line 172) — special-cases the wildcard-with-credentials combination via UnsafeWildcardOriginWithAllowCredentials, reflecting the actual Origin when '*' + AllowCredentials are both set.

questions.question: This backend's entire handler, middleware, form, and plugin surface programs against a single large abstraction (roughly 100 methods) rather than a concrete type, and there is exactly one production implementor. Name that interface, its file, and the concrete struct that satisfies it.
questions.gold_answer: The core.App interface in core/app.go is the single abstraction the whole backend depends on (its ~100 methods include DB() dbx.Builder, Save, OnRecordCreate, etc.). Its sole production implementor is the BaseApp struct, asserted with `var _ App = (*BaseApp)(nil)` in core/base.go. ~158 non-test references to core.App across the tree (e.g. NewRouter(app core.App), Serve(app core.App, ...)).

questions.question: Every OnXxx lifecycle event in this backend (record create/update, bootstrap, serve, realtime subscribe, ...) ultimately dispatches through one generic method that snapshots its handler slice, builds a reverse-order chain, and invokes it. Name that method, the generic type it hangs off, and its file.
questions.gold_answer: Hook[T].Trigger in tools/hook/hook.go (line 153). It copies h.handlers plus any one-off funcs, then iterates from the end building nested nextFunc closures (reverse order) and finally calls event.Next() to run the chain front-to-back. TaggedHook[T].Trigger (tools/hook/tagged.go) is the tag-filtering wrapper. ~97 non-test .Trigger( call sites (OnRecordCreate().Trigger, OnRealtimeSubscribeRequest().Trigger, ...).

questions.question: All of this backend's HTTP API routes are wired in one constructor: it builds the router, opens a single '/api' group, and calls a sequence of bindXxxApi installers (settings, collections, record CRUD, auth, logs, backup, cron, file, batch, realtime, health). Name that function and its file.
questions.gold_answer: NewRouter in apis/base.go (line 19) constructs router.Router[*core.RequestEvent], creates `apiGroup := pbRouter.Group("/api")`, and calls the 11 binders bindSettingsApi, bindCollectionApi, bindRecordCrudApi, bindRecordAuthApi, bindLogsApi, bindBackupApi, bindCronApi, bindFileApi, bindBatchApi, bindRealtimeApi, bindHealthApi. It is called once from apis/serve.go.

questions.question: Persistence in this backend flows through one accessor that hands back a builder which silently routes read queries (SELECT) to one connection and writes (Model/insert) to another, to keep SQLite from blocking. Name the accessor method, the wrapper type it returns, and the two underlying builders it dispatches between.
questions.gold_answer: BaseApp.DB() (core/base.go, line 490) returns `&dualDBBuilder{concurrentDB, nonconcurrentDB}`. The dualDBBuilder (core/db_builder.go) sends Select(...) to concurrentDB and Model(...) (writes) to nonconcurrentDB; callers can also reach ConcurrentDB()/NonconcurrentDB() directly. ~58 non-test .DB() call sites route through it.

questions.question: A single function boots this backend's HTTP server for every production launch: it runs all pending migrations, constructs the router, binds CORS and static file serving, then starts listening. Name it, its file, and the config struct it accepts.
questions.gold_answer: Serve(app core.App, config ServeConfig) in apis/serve.go (line 61). It calls app.RunAllMigrations(), builds the router with NewRouter(app), then pbRouter.Bind(CORS(...)) plus gzip/static wiring before listening. ServeConfig (apis/serve.go, line 28) carries AllowedOrigins, HttpAddr, etc.

questions.question: Every schema field type in this backend (text, number, relation, file, json, geoPoint, ...) implements one interface and is constructed through one global factory map keyed by type name. Name that interface, the registry map, and their file.
questions.gold_answer: The Field interface (core/field.go, line 70) is implemented by all ~14 concrete field types (TextField, NumberField, RelationField, FileField, JSONField, GeoPointField, ...). They register into the global `var Fields = map[string]FieldFactoryFunc{}` (core/field.go, line 67), whose values are FieldFactoryFunc constructors keyed by Type() string.

questions.question: Use the lci_semantic_annotations tool with category 'api-routing'. Which function is annotated as the API route installer, what route group does the annotation name, and where is it defined?
questions.gold_answer: The api-routing annotation points to NewRouter in apis/base.go. Its route_group tag is `/api`, and the annotation describes NewRouter as installing the backend API route groups.
